landmarks.landmark_blurb: Held British Loyalist prisoners here during the Revolution.

notable.notable_blurb: Freed enslaved Bristow before enlisting; Siege of Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Died of pleurisy at Boston — unmarked grave
narrative_blocks.body: He was among the first to enlist at Farmington in 1775 and joined the army near Boston. He died of pleurisy on November 26, 1775, age 30, during the Siege of Boston — buried in an unmarked grave at Roxbury. EH wrote: "Today the ashes of those patriot dead rest peacefully beneath the wild turmoil of Boston's streets."

narrative_blocks.header: Freed Bristow days before enlisting — £60 paid
narrative_blocks.body: On May 9, 1775 — days before leaving for war — he signed a Deed of Manumission freeing Bristow, an African man age 44, who paid £60 (roughly 400 days' wages) for his freedom. The deed reads: "he may go & come when & wherever he pleases." Bristow later willed his estate to Thomas's children. A West Hartford middle school was named Bristow Middle School in 2005 — believed to be the first Connecticut school named for a formerly enslaved person.

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother Roger survived — rose to Major
narrative_blocks.body: He and his half-brother Roger Hooker — different mothers; Roger's was Anna Kellogg, his was Mercy Hart — both answered the Revolution's first call. Thomas died of disease in 1775; Roger rose to Major, carried news of Burgoyne's surrender to the British, and outlived the war by 55 years.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Abigail Pantry Hooker married Samuel Talcott, uniting the Hooker and Talcott founding-family lines of Hartford. This is the compiler's primary point of double descent from Thomas Hooker and the Talcott family simultaneously.

narrative_blocks.header: West Hartford home now a National Register site
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hart built his West Hartford home in 1770 — the Sarah Whitman Hooker Homestead at the corner of South Main Street and New Britain Avenue, where his widow Sarah continued to live and raise their children after his death. The house stands on the National Register of Historic Places, named for her.

documents.document_blurb: Summons to appear before CT General Assembly, Woodruff v. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.unit: Suffolk County Militia, Massachusetts Militia; Connecticut volunteers from Farmington
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call 1775. "One of the first to enlist." Joined army near Boston; died of pleurisy Nov 26, 1775 during Siege of Boston. Buried in unmarked grave at Roxbury MA.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hart" commemorates his mother Mercy Hart

documents.document_blurb: Summons to appear before CT General Assembly, Woodruff v. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.unit: Suffolk County Militia, Massachusetts Militia; Connecticut volunteers from Farmington
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call 1775. "One of the first to enlist." Joined army near Boston; died of pleurisy Nov 26, 1775 during Siege of Boston. Buried in unmarked grave at Roxbury MA.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hart" commemorates his mother Mercy Hart

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather succeeded Rev. Samuel Hooker at Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather Rev. Samuel Whitman succeeded Rev. Samuel Hooker as minister at Farmington — making her family already entwined with the Hooker line before her marriage. Rev. Samuel Whitman's mother Anna Alcock was a granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker's sister.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed in 1775 — remarried four years later
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Thomas Hart Hooker died of pleurisy during the Siege of Boston on November 26, 1775. On April 8, 1779 she married Capt. Seth Collins of West Hartford as his second wife. She died at West Hartford on June 5, 1837.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: She lived here with first husband Thomas Hart Hooker, then with second husband Capt. Seth Collins.

classification.lineage_note: Thomas¹ → Samuel²(H00007) → John³ → Roger⁴ → Thomas⁵ → Abigail⁶ Pantry Hooker
classification.intersection_note: Descends from Thomas Hooker (paternal) AND marries into Talcott line (T01389). Compiler descends from her through Talcott line.

compiler_connection.notes: Abigail Pantry Hooker is the compiler's generation 6 ancestor on the maternal Talcott line. Her marriage to Samuel Talcott (T01389) on Jan 25, 1795 is the Hooker-Talcott intersection that gives the compiler his middle name 'Talcott'. Her son Seth Talcott (H01789) → John Butler Talcott (H03203) → Florence H. Talcott → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: HD0064 Abigail Pantry Hooker × T01389 Samuel Talcott → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

open_questions.question: EH entry number for Abigail Pantry Hooker
open_questions.notes: Talcott 1876 identifies her as 5th generation from Thomas Hooker. EH entry number unknown — currently HD0064. Once found, rename to H#####.
open_questions.action: Search EH for children of Thomas Hart Hooker (who held Skene prisoners, LM005); find Abigail Pantry Hooker entry

primary_residence.notes: Born West Hartford; lived there with husband Samuel Talcott after 1788; died there 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Hooker-Talcott convergence in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: On January 25, 1795 she married Samuel Talcott of Glastonbury. She was a fifth-generation Hooker; he was a fifth-generation Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father's freed man left her his estate
narrative_blocks.body: Bristow had been freed by her father Thomas Hart Hooker at the news of Lexington in April 1775. His 1791 will bequeathed his estate to her and her brother.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died at Roxbury in the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: He freed his enslaved man Bristow at news of the Battle of Lexington and enlisted with a Farmington volunteer company. He sickened and died near Boston in winter 1775-76, buried at Roxbury.

narrative_blocks.header: Eighty-four years in West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: She was born and died in West Hartford, where she stayed for the entirety of her marriage and widowhood. She is buried in Old Center Cemetery beside her husband.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms. Buried with husband T01389 Samuel Talcott III.

bio.notes: 'Pantry' appears to be a middle name, possibly a family name. EH entry number unknown — using HD prefix until confirmed.

compiler_connection.notes: Samuel Talcott's marriage to Abigail Pantry Hooker is the key Talcott-Hooker intersection in the compiler's maternal line. Their son Seth (H01789) → John Butler (H03203) → Florence → Helen → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: T01389 Samuel × HD0064 Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John B. → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

primary_residence.notes: Resided in Eastbury (Glastonbury) until 1788, then West Hartford for the rest of his life.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms. Buried with wife H00687 Abigail Pantry Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Hooker-Talcott convergence in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: On January 25, 1795 he married Abigail Pantry Hooker in West Hartford. He was a fifth-generation Talcott; she was a fifth-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved from Glastonbury to West Hartford in 1788
narrative_blocks.body: He left the Talcott homeland in Eastbury for West Hartford seven years before his marriage. He stayed there for the rest of his life.

burial.plot_notes: Bridgehampton Cemetery, Bridgehampton, Suffolk County NY. FindAGrave confirms. Died 22 days after her son Samuel was born — possible death in childbirth or post-partum complications.

narrative_blocks.header: Died 22 days after her son Samuel was
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza Talcott Francis married Rev. Amzi Francis on May 10, 1824 in West Hartford and moved with him to Bridgehampton, Long Island. Her son Samuel Talcott Francis was born July 26, 1829. Twenty-two days later, on August 17, Eliza died at age 33.

narrative_blocks.header: Sampler records the whole family in silk thread
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1815, when she was about nineteen, Eliza stitched a needlework sampler recording the births of her parents and all six siblings — including a note in her own hand: "my dear little brother John died Oct 17 1815 aged 4 years and 9 months." The sampler is held by the Connecticut Historical Society in Hartford.

cross_connections.display_label: Married his cousin Cynthia Olcott of the Roger Hooker line

burial.plot_notes: North Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived three wives in West Hartford Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married three times and outlived two of them. Cynthia Olcott — his Hooker-line cousin — died at twenty-three the year after their wedding. Lucelia Brace bore him seven children before dying in 1839.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his Hooker first cousin once removed
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's mother Abigail Pantry Hooker descended from Thomas Hooker through his son Samuel; his first wife Cynthia Olcott descended from Thomas Hooker through Roger Hooker. Their marriage in 1822 united two parallel Hooker lines that had run separately for five generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost five children before they reached adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Hooker died in infancy in 1827. Sarah died at thirteen in 1851. Oscar Llewellyn died at seven in 1852.

compiler_connection.notes: Seth Talcott is the compiler's direct Talcott ancestor generation 7. His son H03203 John Butler Talcott → Florence H. → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence H. → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler

primary_residence.notes: Born and died West Hartford. Charlotte his widow died Hartford Jan 4, 1875.

burial.plot_notes: North Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Named three of seven children for Hooker ancestry
narrative_blocks.body: Seth Talcott Sr. and Charlotte Stout Butler had seven children. Among them: Seth Jr. (father of painter Allen Butler Talcott), and John B. Talcott — who would found the New Britain Museum of American Art through the Talcott Art Fund.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived eighty-six years unmarried in West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Talcott, daughter of Samuel Talcott III and Abigail Pantry Hooker, never married. She lived in West Hartford her entire life, born 1806 and dying January 18, 1893 at age 86 — outliving all her siblings.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost an eight-year-old on her birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and Elizabeth's third child Abigail Hooker Talcott was born April 15, 1848 and died on her eighth birthday, April 15, 1856. The Henry Talcott household lost this daughter in West Hartford the same year his elder brother Sam IV's daughter Sarah Whiting Talcott had also died at thirteen, only five years earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Bull — daughter of Thomas Bull, a Hartford Founder
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Josiah Hart of Farmington — son of John Hart, grandson of Stephen Hart (X00034)

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.

served Hartford’s founding-family public life alongside him
was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother

Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
